calendar.matters:
Petitioners Motion To Strike And Tax Tpg Capital Management L.P.S Memorandum Of Costs
Off Calendar May-04-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PETITIONER JASON HEYMAN, MARTIN LESAK, DOMINIC NUCIFORO's Motion To Strike And Tax Tpg Capital Management L.P.

Heyman's motion to tax is GRANTED IN PART. The court strikes $945.16 in travel expenses. "The only travel expenses authorized by section 1033.5 are those to attend depositions. ( 1033.5, subd. (a)(3).)" ( Ladas v. CSAA (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 775.) TPG's distinction between local and non-local travel expenses does not change this rule. The court denies the motion as to the e-filing fees, which TPG substantiates in its opposition. The court denies Heyman's motion to tax the entire costs memo because it was served by e-mail. Heyman received the memo and was able to attack it. In addition, the court plans to vacate Heyman's dismissal without prejudice and enter a new judgment for dismissal with prejudice. TPG would be able to file and mail-serve a new costs memorandum after entry of the new dismissal. "The law neither does nor requires idle acts" and the court addresses the merits of the claimed costs. (CC 3532.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano , CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. (D302/RBU)

MOTION FOR ENTRY OF JUDGMENT
Off Calendar May-04-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

RESPONDENT TPG CAPITAL MANAGEMENT, L.P.'s MOTION FOR ENTRY OF JUDGMENT AND ATTORNEY'S FEES.

TPG's motion to vacate the voluntary dismissal without prejudice entered on March 8, 2018 is GRANTED. This case concerned the propriety of the subpoena and when the court denied the motion to compel, the case was effectively over. At that point, a judgment of dismissal with prejudice in favor of TPG was inevitable and Heyman's right to file a voluntary dismissal was cut off because the "trial" already commenced within the meaning of CCP 581. (Law Offices of Andre L. Ellis v. Yang (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 869, 877.) TPG shall prepare, file, and serve a judgment of dismissal with prejudice in its favor against petitioners forthwith. TPG's request for fess under CCP 1987.2 is DENIED. First, TPG sought expenses when opposing the discovery motion and the court issued an order that did not award fees. TPG is effectively bringing an improper motion for reconsideration that does not comply with CCP 1008. Second, the court finds that Heyman acted with substantial justification and therefore, an award of fees is not warranted.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano , CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. (D302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion For Order Compelling Compliance With Deposition Subpoena In Arbitration
Off Calendar Jan-02-2018 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: PETITIONER JASON HEYMAN, MARTIN LESAK, DOMINIC NUCIFORO Notice Of Motion For Order Compelling Compliance With Deposition Subpoena In Arbitration

Notice Of Motion For Order Compelling Compliance With Deposition Subpoena In Arbitration
Off Calendar Feb-22-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Sean Hardy, Esq. 310-201-0005 of Freedman & Taitelman for Petitioner Jason Heyman through Court Call.
F. Philip Hosp, Esq. 213-687-6787 of Locke Lord LLP for Respondent TPG Capital.

RULING - A R G U E D. Petitioner Argues. The Court elaborates on the tentative ruling. Respondent Argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PETITIONER JASON HEYMAN, MARTIN LESAK, DOMINIC NUCIFORO's Motion For Order Compelling Compliance With Deposition Subpoena In Arbitration.

Petitioners move to compel compliance with a putative deposition subpoena to non-party TPG Capital Management, L.P. in an arbitration. "Only if the parties by the agreement so provide" may such broad discovery be had in an arbitration. (CCP 1283.1(b).) Petitioners rely on Rule 17 of the JAMS Employment Arbitration Rules & Procedure (2014), which are provided for in the arbitration agreement. However, JAMS Rule 17 regards discovery between parties to the arbitration, as demonstrated by its nine uses of the capitalized word "Parties" (defined as "Parties to the Arbitration"). In contrast, the terms "third party," "non-party" and "deposition subpoena" appear nowhere in Rule 17. Additionally, while petitioners fail to set out the document demands and responses as required by California discovery law, their demands are clearly overbroad and burdensome. The motion is DENIED. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. (D302/RBU)
